System Design

SystemDesign Cashing

System Design (cashing)

Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading): Memcached and Redis

🔍 What is "Peak Load"?

Peak load refers to the maximum level of demand on a system — typically when the number of users or requests is at its highest.

Read-Through Cache:

Read-through cache sits in-line with the database. When there is a cache miss, it loads missing data from database, populates the cache and returns it to the application.

Write-Through Cache:

In this write strategy, data is first written to the cache and then to the database. The cache sits in-line with the database and writes always go through the cache to the main database. This helps cache maintain consistency with the main database.

1. Strong Consistency

  • When you read data, you are guaranteed to get the most recent write.
  • No stale data.
  • Slower, because it may need to coordinate across nodes or wait for replication.

2. Eventual Consistency

  • When you read data, you might get stale data.
  • The system will eventually become consistent, but not immediately.
  • Faster, because it avoids coordination delays.

Write-Around (bypassed):

Here, data is written directly to the database and only the data that is read makes its way into the cache.

Write-Back or Write-Behind

Here, the application writes data to the cache which stores the data and acknowledges to the application immediately. Then later, the cache writes the data back to the database.
This is very similar to Write-Through but there’s one crucial difference: In Write-Through, the data written to the cache is synchronously updated in the main database. In Write-Back, the data written to the cache is asynchronously updated in the main database. From the application perspective, writes to Write-Back caches are faster because only the cache needed to be updated before returning a response.

🔄 Caching Strategies: Lazy vs Bypassed

Strategy

Lazy Loading?

Bypasses Cache on Write?

Notes

Cache-Aside

✅ Yes

✅ Yes

App loads data into cache only when needed (lazy). Writes go directly to DB, then optionally update cache.

Read-Through

✅ Yes

❌ No

Cache loads data on read if missing (lazy). Writes go through cache, which updates DB.

Write-Through

❌ No

❌ No

Writes go to cache, which immediately writes to DB. Cache is always updated.

Write-Behind

❌ No

❌ No (but async)

Writes go to cache, which asynchronously writes to DB later. Cache is updated immediately.

Write-Around

✅ Yes (on read)

✅ Yes

Writes go directly to DB. Cache is updated only if data is read later (lazy).

Key Differences:

Feature

GeoDNS-Routed

Load Balancer

Routing Layer

DNS level (before connection)

Network/application level (after connection)

Decision Based On

User’s geographic location

Server health, load, session, etc.

Use Case

Route users to nearest data center

Distribute traffic among servers in a data center

Example

Route EU users to Frankfurt DC

Balance traffic between Server A and B in Frankfurt
